title: Review of Small Modular Reactors: Challenges in Safety and Economy to Success | Korean Journal of Chemical Engineering | Springer Nature Link
text:
Abstract
This paper explores the paradigm shift in nuclear energy generation from large, centralized nuclear power plants towards small modular reactors (SMRs), in response to the evolving economic, safety, and environmental challenges faced by the nuclear industry. For over 70 years, nuclear power has been a cornerstone of electricity generation, offering a low-carbon, reliable, and cost-effective solution. However, the traditional model of large-scale nuclear reactors has encountered significant barriers, including high financial risks, stringent safety concerns, and a lack of adaptability to smaller electrical grids, and changing energy markets. With over 70 types of SMRs currently under development globally, this study reviews several prominent models to assess their general characteristics and potential advantages. Through simple physical and economic models, the paper evaluates the impact of reducing reactor size on efficiency, safety, and cost, highlighting the shared traits and innovations across different SMR designs. The findings indicate that SMRs could address many of the limitations of conventional nuclear power, offering more flexible, scalable, and economically viable options for electricity generation. This paper finally tries to identify key technologies and strategic considerations essential for their successful deployment in a rapidly transforming global energy landscape.
Similar content being viewed by others
Introduction
Nuclear energy has been used for electricity generation over 70 years. During this time, the nuclear power has played an important role for supplying electricity cheaply, reliably, and securely while reducing greenhouse gas emission. Countries able to install nuclear power plants utilized nuclear power for baseload application mostly, since if nuclear power is available, the nuclear power was the cheapest among other energy sources [1].
It is shown throughout the history that the main development strategy to make nuclear energy more economical was by increasing the capacity of a single power plant. This was also true for other power plants such as coal and gas power plants. This is because the business of power plants strongly follows the economy of scale. Even though the price for single power plant will increase but the cost of producing the final product, which is electricity for power plant, will decrease [2, 3].
Thus, the engineer designed a nuclear power plant to increase the capacity as much as possible and it was mainly limited by the manufacturing capability. If the supply chain could have allowed larger component manufacturing, the capacity of single nuclear power plant may have increased more than now. This direction to develop technology for increasing the single unit capacity is the main characteristic of the second-generation nuclear reactor [4].
However, the strategy to increase the single unit capacity of nuclear power plant also brought a few shortcomings [5]. Since one reactor stores a large amount of radioactive material due to the increased capacity, the radiological consequence of a severe accident for one reactor became larger. Even though the cost of electricity production was low, the total financial burden to construct one large nuclear power plant was too large to initiate the project. Furthermore, only countries with large grid capacity can host such large power plants, and this also acts as a limitation to many countries wanting to start the nuclear power program.
The energy market is also diversifying and becoming more and more reliant on the private sector funding due to the increased penetration of renewable energy sources. This also poses a challenge to not only nuclear power sector but also to all power generation sectors which rely on large power plants for economic production of electricity [6]. At the same time, the harmonization of nuclear and renewable is needed for the successful decarbonization of human society [7, 8].
Due to the technical shortcomings of a large nuclear power plant and the change in energy market environment, a new form of utilizing nuclear energy was suggested. Furthermore, the nuclear energy system should become more flexible to operate in the grid with deep penetration of variable renewable energy sources [8, 10]. This new approach is to develop a SMR technology. Currently, more than 70 different types of SMR are being developed, and there are a lot of good reports publicly available, which summarize SMRs under development around the world [11, 12, 14].
In this paper, a selected number of SMRs will be reviewed first, which are seemed to be relevant not only to South Korean nuclear industry but also global nuclear industry. This will be followed by discussing the general characteristics of SMR and evaluate the generally accepted characteristics of SMR with simple physical/economic models. The evaluation aims to show the effect of size reduction on nuclear energy system, and what shared genuine characteristics can be observed from diverse types of SMRs under development. Furthermore, the result of the simplified evaluation can shed light to the technical direction of SMRs to be developed. Thus, the necessary technologies to accelerate deployment of SMRs and to become successful in the fast-changing energy market can be identified and suggested.
Review of SMRs Under Development
Generations of Nuclear Reactors and SMRs
The generation of nuclear reactors can be classified with the following aspects [15]. The Generation I nuclear reactors are the ones developed for prototype and pilot scale nuclear reactors, which the aim is to prove concept, develop engineering process, and assess if the reactor can become successful energy source in the market.
The Generation II nuclear reactors are the commercial nuclear power plants which consist most of the current operating fleet around the world. The major characteristics are large capacity and producing cheap electricity for baseload. Some countries standardized Generation II nuclear power plants to further lower the cost by minimizing the cost of engineering.
The Generation III nuclear reactors are the nuclear reactors designed after experiencing severe accidents such as Three Mile Island Unit 2 accident or Chernobyl accident. Therefore, on top of the Generation II technologies, the Generation III nuclear reactors focused more on safety. Both Generation II and III technologies were dominated by the light-water reactor types such as pressurized water reactors and boiling water reactors. The Generation III nuclear reactors are being constructed around the world and many of them already started the operation. Generation III reactors can be further classified as Generation III+ nuclear reactors. Generation III+ reactors are the ones with better safety by adopting passive safety systems more than the Generation III reactors. Examples of the Generation III/III+ nuclear reactors are APR1400 of Korean consortium, AP1000 of Westinghouse, ESBWR of General Electric, EPR of Framatome, and VVER of Rosatom.
The Generation IV technologies are the ones currently being researched and developed around the world, and thought as the next-generation nuclear reactors following the Generation III technologies. The main characteristics of the Generation IV nuclear reactors are to move away from the light-water reactor technologies to enable nuclear energy to be more sustainable, safe, and economical. Therefore, liquid metal-cooled, gas-cooled, and molten salt reactors are included in the Generation IV nuclear reactors (Fig. 1).
However, it is interesting that the SMR technology is not categorized in the generation of nuclear reactors [12]. This means that SMR does not represent a specific type of nuclear reactor, rather it is a name for a new nuclear energy product that is different from existing large nuclear power plants. Thus, any Generation III or Generation IV nuclear reactors aiming to be small and modular are in fact an SMR. According to the international atomic energy agency (IAEA), small reactor is a reactor type that has a power capacity below 300 MWe.
The SMRs can be largely classified as light-water (H2O) cooled reactor (LWR)-based SMR technologies and non-LWR-based SMR technologies. LWR SMRs are being developed from the well-established large nuclear power plant technologies. Non-LWR-based SMRs utilize coolant ranging from liquid metal (e.g., sodium) and gas (e.g., helium) to molten salts (e.g., Flibe). It is noted that the following brief review of SMRs are based on the summary of publications prepared by the International Atomic Energy Agency [11] and the Korean Nuclear Society [12, 14].
LWR SMRs
SMART
The Korean SMR SMART was one of the first licensed SMRs based on LWR technology in western world. The development commenced in 1997, completing its basic design and validation, and in 2012, it became the world’s first integral type PWR type SMR to obtain standard design approval. In 2018, the design for the pre-construction phase (PPE) of a pilot reactor in Saudi Arabia was completed, including the drafting of a preliminary safety analysis report. In 2019, the Korea–Saudi Arabia joint consortium applied for standard design approval for the construction of the SMART pilot plant in Saudi Arabia and for additional exports to third countries based on the PPE project outcomes. The integral type PWR has the main characteristic of eliminating piping to connect major nuclear components so that the system can become more compact, and being exempt from large break loss of coolant accident (LBLOCA), which greatly improves safety. The design of SMART has been further improved to incorporate more passive safety systems than the previous version, and the revised design of SMART is named as SMART100. Due to the safety system design change, SMART100 standard design certificate is newly applied and currently under review by the Korean licensing body (Fig. 2).
NuScale Power Module
The integral type PWR configuration was also adopted by NuScale Power Module. The NRC completed its first design certification for a 50 MWe module in 2020. Projects are underway for the construction and technical cooperation of NuScale with Canada and several European countries. In 2020, the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) approved budget in construction support funds, with a goal to operate NuScale's first module at the Idaho National Laboratory (INL) site by 2029. Preliminary activities for the Standard Design Approval (SDA) application for a 77 MWe module to the US Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) and the progress of Canadian Nuclear Safety Commission (CNSC) Vendor Design Review (VDR) Phase 1 is finished.
A few notable technology advancements can be observed in NuScale. First, multi-module placement in one building was suggested. NuScale suggested a plant layout which shows that up to 12 modules are placed in the power plant, and each module generates around 50 MW with an independent steam turbine. Moreover, all modules are placed under the water providing ample of cooling water to the containment vessel passively, which greatly improves the safety. Second, all 12 modules can be controlled with the minimum number of staffs. If each module was treated as a nuclear power plant, then each module should be controlled by a group of operators. However, NuScale has successfully demonstrated to the regulatory body of the US that 12 modules can be treated as one nuclear power plant, and therefore, the number of operators can be minimized. Third, it adopted full power natural circulation operating scheme so that the active pump components can be removed from the module simplifying the system configuration. Combining all these aspects, NuScale design was certified by the US NRC, and it was also allowed that the emergency planning zone can be reduced to the plant site boundary, a few hundred meters, as compared to one which used to be 10 mile radius for large nuclear power plants. Recently, the power for one module is being increased to 77 MWe, which can reduce the cost (Fig. 3).
ACP100
The People’s Republic of China is developing an SMR based on integral type PWR, namely ACP100. The development was initiated 2010, and it was centered around the China National Nuclear Corporation (CNNC). It passed the International Atomic Energy Agency's (IAEA) Generic Reactor Safety Review, becoming the first in the world to do so in 2016. In 2021, the National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC) of China approved the construction plan for the ACP100 demonstration reactor. The construction work on the world's first commercial terrestrial pressurized water SMR, named Linglong No. 1, began in 2021 in the Changjiang area of Hainan Province, southern China. The installation of the bottom part of the containment vessel, which is 15 m tall and weighs 450 tons, was completed in 2022 (Fig. 4).
NUWARD
NUWARD is an SMR with 170 MWe power capacity, integral type PWR, which is currently being developed by a consortium led by Électricité de France (EDF). The conceptual design began in 2019. In 2022, Tractebel, a global company of Belgian nationality founded in 1862, known for providing consulting and engineering services in various fields including nuclear energy, joined the conceptual design team. Nuclear regulatory agencies from France (ASN), Czech Republic (SUJB), and Finland (STUK) selected NUWARD for cooperative licensing review. The goal is to complete the basic design by 2025, and the construction of a demonstration reactor is targeted to begin in 2030 (Fig. 5).
i-SMR
Recently a Korean industrial consortium initiated a new SMR development project, and the name of the SMR is innovative SMR (in short “i-SMR”). A commercialization strategy is to obtain the standard design approval by 2028, starting the first unit project in 2030, leading to the initial commercial operation of an i-SMR by 2034. From 2021 to 2023, Korea Hydro & Nuclear Power is leading the consortium including nuclear research institutes, and major private companies for the concept design and basic design of the i-SMR. The i-SMR aims to achieve the Core Damage Frequency (CDF) 1000 times lower compared to large nuclear power plants. It features a boron-free core design that reduces the chemical and volume control system (CVCS) and liquid waste, and the application of an in-vessel control rod drive mechanism (CRDM) eliminates the possibility of control rod ejection accidents. Compared to large nuclear power plants, the initial investment cost is lower, expanding its utilization range as a new source of power for countries with small power grids, as well as replacing old coal power plants, and serving as a source of distributed generation in isolated, coastal, and island regions. Moreover, the process heat generated by each module can be utilized for desalination, district heating, and hydrogen production. The i-SMR design integrates the reactor coolant system (RCS) into a single unit, eliminating the need for large connecting pipes between components. The RCS consists of a core with 69 nuclear fuel assemblies, four reactor coolant pumps (RCPs), a steam generator with a helical tube structure, and a pressurizer (PZR), all of which are installed within a single reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and enclosed within a steel containment vessel (CV). This integrated design inherently eliminates the possibility of a LBLOCA by removing the large pipes that could fail and lead to such an incident.
The distinctive feature of i-SMR compared to NuScale is the containment cooling system. The containment of i-SMR is passively cooled via containment passive cooling system and heat exchanger trains instead of submerging the whole system under water pool as in NuScale. This is to utilize the already developed large nuclear reactor passive cooling system as much as possible to reduce the uncertainty in the development. Furthermore, i-SMR targets to achieve the levelized cost of electricity (LCOE) 65 USD/MWh, and construction overnight cost of 3500 USD/kWe, which the values are comparable and competitive to those of a large nuclear power plant (Fig. 6).
Another technical approach for PWR type SMR is to directly connecting major components nozzle to nozzle. Since major components of PWR can be all categorized as a pressure vessel, if the nozzle on each pressure vessel is connected to each other without piping, it will have the similar safety feature as the integral reactor but the size of the largest pressure vessel can be reduced, and more flexibility in the configuration can be expected. This type of SMR is specifically categorized as ‘block-type’ SMR. The next three block-type PWR SMRs are example of such SMR type.
KLT-40S
The KLT-40S is a block-type PWR SMR used in Russia's first floating nuclear power plant, the Akademik Lomonosov, developed by OKBM Afrikantov and manufactured by NMZ (Nizhniy Novgorod Machine Engineering Plant). Reactors were installed in 2009, and the Akademik Lomonosov was launched on 2010. In 2019, it was transferred to Rosatom, the Russian government’s nuclear power company, and moved to its permanent mooring site in the Chukotka region. The floating nuclear power plant Akademik Lomonosov, connected to the Chaun-Bilibino power grid in the far eastern region of Pevek, began producing electricity, becoming the world's first SMR to do so in 2019. The ‘Akademik Lomonosov’ floating nuclear power plant is equipped with two 35 MWe class marine PWR SMRs, marking the world’s first commercial operation of its kind. It adopts a partially passive safety system that combines passive and active cooling systems, which could potentially reduce its ability to respond during a power outage (Fig. 7).
BANDI-60
The BANDI-60 is being developed by KEPCO E&C in South Korea, and it is characterized by a block-type PWR SMR that connects the reactor and the steam generator directly nozzle-to-nozzle without the need for large piping. Niche markets are targeted rather than large-scale power markets, such as power supply for islands or remote areas, heating/process heat, desalination supply, and propulsion for commercial ships. It aims for early market entry through the optimal use of proven pressurized water reactor technology. The key features are as follows: for enhanced safety, advanced technologies such as a boron-free core, built-in control rod drive mechanism, and a modular design concept are introduced. A modular PWR type SMR is designed to eliminate LBLOCA. It aims for design simplification and streamlining with a boron-free core design and eliminates control rod ejection accidents with a built-in control rod drive mechanism design. This fundamentally eliminates corrosion issues caused by boric acid and simplifies design by removing the complex boron system. A completely passive design is adopted to ensure the reactor’s safety without the need for external power or operator actions. Top-mounted In-core Instrumentation design is also adopted to improve safety, which can exclude accidents involving damage to instruments at the bottom of the reactor vessel due to core melt during severe accidents (Fig. 8).
SMR-160/300
A block-type PWR SMR is being actively developed by Holtec International. The company has experiences in manufacturing equipment for the nuclear industry, decommissioning nuclear power plants, and possesses infrastructure for designing and manufacturing SMRs. It is interesting to note that SMR-160 was developed as an earlier version which was a block-type SMR relying on natural circulation with power output of 160 MWe to eliminate pump, but it is now uprated to 300 MWe and named as SMR-300 by adopting booster pump for the forced circulation operation while maintaining similar safety features with SMR-160. It has received a grant from the U.S. Department of Energy’s Advanced Reactor Demonstration Program (ARDP). Currently, the site at the Oyster Creek nuclear power plant in New Jersey is being considered as the construction site for the first unit, which Holtec International is in the process of decommissioning. The pre-application activities for licensing with the US NRC are underway, and Phase 1 VDR by the CNSC is completed (Fig. 9).
Alternative to PWR type LWR, boiling water reactor (BWR) can be also an option for a LWR SMR. BWRX-300 which is developed by General Electric-Hitachi (GEH) is a good example.
BWRX-300
GEH developed the ESBWR technology, which is a large BWR capable of producing 1520 MWe designed to operate with two-phase natural circulation at full power. BWRX-300 utilizes the large nuclear reactor technology, and due to the size reduction to 300 MWe capacity, many passive safety features already developed for ESBWR can be adopted more easily. BWR is very close to being an integral reactor since all major components are in a pressure vessel, making the system simple and compact which can be ideal for SMRs. Ontario Power Generation (OPG) decided to build a BWRX-300 at the Darlington site in Ontario, Canada, by 2028, which can be potentially the fastest SMR to start operation in western countries. Pre-application activities for US NRC licensing and CNSC VDR Phase 2 are also underway (Fig. 10).
Non-LWR SMRs
Non-LWR-type SMRs utilize non-water coolant for transporting nuclear heat to power cycle. Sodium-cooled fast reactor (SFR), lead-cooled fast reactor (LFR), high-temperature gas-cooled reactor (HTGR), and molten salt reactor (MSR) are reviewed in this paper. Since many of non-LWR SMRs are Generation IV technologies, operating experiences are less with these SMR technologies compared to LWR SMRs. Moreover, this also implies larger uncertainties and greater challenges in licensing compared to LWR SMRs. Non-LWR SMRs are typically adopting Generation IV technologies and, therefore, it should aim to achieve comparable or better economy than Generation III/III+-based SMRs. Furthermore, since non-LWR SMRs are typically designed to achieve higher temperature than LWR-based SMRs, the core outlet temperature is in the range of 500–900 ºC. Furthermore, for the SMRs utilizing liquid metal or molten salts typically operate near atmospheric pressure, 1 bar, which can potentially eliminate high-pressure accident scenarios in most of design basis accidents.
SFR
Natrium™
Since 2019, TerraPower has begun joint development of the Natrium™ reactor with GEH. The Natrium™ reactor, a sodium-cooled reactor combined with a molten salt energy storage system, can increase its output from a base load of 345 MWe to over 500 MWe for more than 5.5 h during peak demand. Its total energy storage capacity is known to be 850 MWh. In 2020, it was selected as a beneficiary of the US DOE’s ARDP, receiving initial funding to construct a Natrium™ reactor demonstration plant. In 2021, TerraPower announced plans to proceed with the Natrium™ reactor demonstration project at the site of a closed coal power plant in Wyoming. Work is underway related to the final site agreement, permits, licensing, and support, with plans to submit the construction permit application to the US NRC.
ARC-100
ARC-100 is a 100 MWe sodium-cooled fast reactor (SFR) developed by Advanced Reactor Concepts. It is developed based on Argonne National Lab’s EBR-II (20 MWe), which operated from 1965 to 2005. The nuclear fuel used is metallic uranium, designed for a reload cycle of over 20 years, with used nuclear fuel designed to be recycled and reused. In 2018, ARC-100 was selected as one of the reactor technologies to be developed in New Brunswick with a commercial operation target for 2030. The deployment of ARC-100 is expected to take place between 2027 and 2030, with the initial reactor core anticipated to be transported to the plant site by the end of 2028. In 2017, ARC-100 applied for the Canadian CNSC VDR, completing Phase 1 of the VDR in 2019 (Fig. 11).
Aurora
In 2019, Oklo announced the development of the Aurora, a micro-nuclear reactor utilizing a compact fast reactor (liquid metal-cooled fast reactor) with an output of 1.5 MWe, known as a fission battery. Aurora has an electrical output of 1.5 MWe and can also produce heat, using metallic fuel. The nuclear fuel replacement cycle is 20 years, with the possibility of fuel recycling. The core heat is transferred to the secondary system via heat pipes, and the plant also aims to demonstrate the integration of nuclear and renewable energy by attaching solar panels to the roof of the power plant building. The site required for the power plant construction is estimated to be less than 1,000 m2. Oklo also obtained a site use permit from the US DOE to build Aurora on an INL site, planning to construct the first Aurora unit by the mid-2020s. A land purchase agreement was signed to build two Aurora power plants, now uprated to 15 MWe each, on a site near Portsmouth, southern Ohio. Moreover, the US Air Force selected Oklo as the supplier for a microreactor to be constructed at Eielson Air Force Base in Alaska in collaboration with the Defense Logistics Agency Energy (DLA Energy), responsible for fuel supply to military facilities (Fig. 12).
LFR
BREST-OD-300
Construction is underway for BREST-OD-300, which will become the world’s first lead-cooled fast reactor demonstration power plant. It is a 300 MW Generation IV lead-cooled fast reactor that uses mixed nitride uranium–plutonium fuel. The BREST power plant is a key part of Russia’s “Breakthrough” project aimed at developing closed nuclear fuel cycle technologies. The Pilot Demonstration Energy Complex (PDEC) under the “Breakthrough” project plans to demonstrate a closed nuclear fuel cycle by connecting three main facilities: a nuclear fuel manufacturing plant, the reactor, and spent nuclear fuel reprocessing facilities. The operational lifespan was previously constrained by corrosion issues. However, the development of new materials has resolved these issues up to 500 °C, allowing the Russian BREST reactor to be designed for 30 years of operation at 535 °C. Consequently, it has received construction approval from regulatory authorities and has commenced construction (Fig. 13).
HTGR
HTR-PM
Construction of the HTR-PM demonstration power plant, a high-temperature gas-cooled reactor, began in 2012 at Shidaowan, Shandong Province, with 100% output operation commencing in 2022. The HTR-PM demonstration power plant is designed with two reactors connected to a single turbine, producing 210 MWe. The construction of the demonstration reactor involves a consortium consisting of Huaneng, China Nuclear Engineering Corporation (CNEC, a subsidiary of CNNC), Tsinghua University, and Chinergy, with Huaneng leading the consortium. The demonstration plant started commissioning in 2020. In 2021, China's National Nuclear Safety Administration (NNSA) issued an operating license for the HTR-PM, a high-temperature gas-cooled demonstration reactor, and this was followed by the nuclear fuel loading. At the Shidaowan site, there are proposals for the construction of an additional 18 HTR-PM reactors, and an extended version called HTR-PM600, which utilizes a single large turbine of 650 MWe powered by six HTR-PM reactors, has been proposed (Fig. 14).
Xe-100
Xe-100 is a pebble-bed-type high-temperature gas-cooled reactor (HTGR) being developed by X-energy, a reactor and nuclear fuel engineering company in the United States. It uses helium as a coolant, with a thermal output of 200 MWt (electric output of 76 MWe). A standard power plant is designed with four reactors installed, producing 300 MWe of electricity. In 2020, it was selected as a recipient of the US DOE's ARDP. X-energy is planning to build a power plant with four Xe-100 reactor modules at the Columbia nuclear power plant site owned by Energy Northwest. In 2023, X-energy and Dow agreed to construct four Xe-100 plants at one of Dow’s Gulf Coast sites. Dow and X-energy plan to start construction of the Xe-100 power plant in 2026 and begin operation by 2033, with plans to prepare and submit a construction permit application to the US NRC. Since 2018, Xe-100 has been undergoing pre-application review by the U.S. NRC, and in 2020, it began the VDR process with the CNSC (Fig. 15).
USNC-MMR
The micro-modular reactor (MMR) is a 5 MWe high-temperature gas-cooled reactor (HTGR) developed by Ultra Safe Nuclear Corporation (USNC), utilizing fully ceramic micro-encapsulated (FCM) fuel. FCM fuel is made by encapsulating nuclear fuel materials such as uranium or plutonium in SiC-coated TRISO particles within a ceramic matrix to form pellets. These ceramic nuclear fuel pellets are loaded into graphite blocks, and helium passes to cool the reactor core. The intermediate heat transfer system, using molten salt, transfers high-temperature heat to either electricity or process heat production systems. In 2021, the provinces of New Brunswick, Ontario, Saskatchewan, and Alberta in Canada proposed using the ultra-small reactor (5 MWe MMR) designed to replace diesel fuel in remote areas and mines. Furthermore, the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign (UIUC) notified the US NRC of its plan to construct USNC's MMR on campus. USNC also participated in the CNSC's VDR, completing Phase 1 (Fig. 16).
MSR
CMSR
Seaborg Technologies, founded in Copenhagen, Denmark, in 2014, is developing a compact molten salt reactor (CMSR) that can be deployed on barges. The CMSR, a thermal neutron spectrum reactor with an electric output of 100 MWe (thermal output of 250 MWth), uses uranium-based fluoride fuel and also serves as the reactor coolant. The CMSR uses molten sodium hydroxide (NaOH) as a moderator and, in conjunction with advanced materials and corrosion control methods, this allows for extending the lifespan of a single CMSR to 12 years. Instead of graphite, molten sodium hydroxide is used as the moderator to prevent radiation damage. However, the potential for molten sodium hydroxide to corrode the reactor vessel and tubes at high temperatures is also being studied, and extensive experimental campaigns on the control of sodium hydroxide’s corrosion are underway. Modular CMSRs are intended for power barges, capable of producing electricity between 200 and 800 MW. They have an operational life of 24 years, with each power module on the barge consisting of two CMSRs. The first two reactors are pre-installed at the shipyard on the power barge, with space for two additional CMSRs in each power module (Fig. 17).
MCFR
TerraPower is developing the Molten Chloride Fast Reactor (MCFR), which uses molten chloride salt as both the reactor coolant and fuel, leveraging fast neutrons. The MCFR operates at higher temperatures than traditional reactors, producing power more efficiently and offering potential for process heat utilization and thermal storage. In 2015, TerraPower and Southern Company received grant from the US DOE to build the integrated infrastructure necessary for the early development of MCFR technology. In 2016, Southern Company and TerraPower began a project through the DOE’s advanced reactor concepts (ARC-15) to construct an integral effect test (IET) facility for MCFR. In 2022, Southern Company and TerraPower completed the construction and installation of a new test facility for the IET necessary for MCFR development at TerraPower’s laboratory in Everett, Washington. The development and operation of the molten chloride reactor experiment (MCRE) at INL are also supported, providing experimental and operational data for reactors under 200 kW. Both the IET and MCRE will provide data for the design, licensing, and operation of a 180 MW MCFR reactor planned for the early 2030s. Southern Company is leading a joint project to build the MCRE at INL, the world’s first fast reactor to reach criticality. MCRE, a demonstration of TerraPower’s MCFR technology, will have a capacity of 500 kW and is planned to be designed, built, and operated within 5 years. MCRE will provide crucial operational data to support the design, licensing, and operation of TerraPower’s future MCFR demonstration reactor. The molten chloride salt circulating in MCRE (36%PuCl3–64%NaCl) acts as both coolant and fuel carrier, containing dissolved fuel. The fission energy raises the temperature of the reactor and coolant, causing the liquid fuel mixture to expand and passively slow down the nuclear fission chain reaction without operator intervention.
KP-FHR
Kairos Power is developing the KP-FHR (Kairos Power Fluoride Salt-Cooled High Temperature Reactor), a 140 MWe molten salt reactor. Hermes is a scaled-down demonstration reactor of KP-FHR. KP-FHR uses fluoride salt coolant and TRISO pebble-bed fuel with a 19.75% enrichment. In 2020, Kairos Power announced plans to deploy Hermes, a test reactor with a thermal output of 35 MWt, at the East Tennessee Technology Park (ETTP), formerly the K-33 gas diffusion enrichment plant site in Oak Ridge, Tennessee, aiming for KP-FHR power production demonstration by 2030. In 2021, as part of the construction permit application for the low-power demonstration reactor Hermes to be built-in Oak Ridge, Tennessee, Kairos submitted the first part of the construction permit application for KP-FHR to the US NRC, and in 2023, the US NRC completed its review of the construction permit application for the Hermes test molten salt reactor and published the Final Safety Evaluation Report (FSER) (Fig. 18).
General Characteristics of SMRs
Why Is “Small” Important?
We can ask a question why “small” is so important in SMRs. A small system implies that the power capacity is small, the physical size is small, and the cost of a unit is small. Small power capacity ensures that an SMR can be constructed and connected to a grid with small size, it can maneuver power easily, and it matches better with variable renewable energy sources which are often a distributed energy source. Furthermore, small power capacity implies that the contained radioactivity in a unit is much smaller than the existing nuclear power plants, and therefore, in case of a severe accident, the radiological consequence can be much smaller.
Small physical size is needed so that the siting flexibility can be improved, transportation of major components can be done via land transportation, and the duration of construction can be shorter. Moreover, since the surface to volume ratio is much higher with an SMR, a passive safety system can be adopted more easily, which simplifies the system, and therefore, ultimately reducing the physical size of an SMR. Passive safety does not only simplify the system but the frequency of accident can be lowered, and therefore, the associated risk of operating SMR becomes minimal since both the consequence and the frequency are reduced simultaneously.
Many utility companies find difficulties to afford a new nuclear power plant since the total construction cost of large nuclear power plants increased substantially in many countries over the years. The uncertainties in the construction cost due to the delayed construction and unresolved licensing issues forbid the utility companies to initiate the nuclear power plant project. Therefore, reducing the cost of an SMR is very important. However, it should be noted that the composition of cost for electricity generation in an SMR will become different from a large nuclear power plant, and therefore, not only the construction cost but also the operation and maintenance (O&M) cost has to be minimized simultaneously for the SMR to be successful.
Is SMR Safer than Large Nuclear Reactor?
The safety of SMR can be assessed in two factors. The first factor is the ability of SMR to reduce the frequency of severe accident. The second factor is the reduction in radiological consequence in case of unlikely event of severe accident. The first factor is often indicated with the ‘core damage frequency’ (i.e., CDF) in the nuclear industry and the second factor can be assessed by the observing the radioactive material concentration at certain distance from the source.
It is a general consensus that CDF of SMRs will be lower than the value for large nuclear reactors since SMRs can rely on passive safety features more easily than the large nuclear reactors. By relying more on the passive safety system, the system can become more simplified, will be less susceptible to the malfunction or failure of active components, and will have less probability of operator making a mistake.
This is argued in terms of physics, since one can quickly observe how reducing the size of reactor actually helps to improve the safety of nuclear reactor. The safety of nuclear reactor is often compromised when the thermal power generated from the reactor cannot be removed from the system due to various expected/unexpected reasons. Heat accumulation will lead to temperature and pressure rise in the system, which will ultimately threaten the integrity of the system. Therefore, one can even over simplify the nuclear safety issue that if the generated heat from the system can be successfully removed, the safety of the nuclear system will be always guaranteed.
It is well known fact that heat removal from the system will depend on three factors; heat transfer coefficient, temperature difference and available surface area for heat transfer. The conventional approach is to improve heat transfer coefficient using active components so that both temperature difference and surface area can be reduced enabling the system to become economical. Passive safety in SMRs is to utilize large available surface area instead of using high heat transfer coefficient, so that the required redundancy and diversity of active components can be eliminated which not only it improves safety and reliability but also economy.
To illustrate how much the total heat transfer will improve compared to the power generated from the nuclear reactor due to the increased available surface area, Fig. 19 is shown. All values are normalized with heat transfer over the surface to power at 100% power level with core power density of large nuclear reactors (e.g., 100 kW/l for pressurized water reactor). The heat transfer is calculated with Eqs. (1)–(3):
Since the heat will be removed most likely in passive means, the overall heat transfer coefficient, U, is assumed to be 20 W/m2-K, which the value is close to the air natural convection heat transfer coefficient, and the temperature difference, \(\Delta T\), is assumed to be 300K, which the value is assumed to represent the temperature difference between the PWR core temperature and the ambient air temperature. With the assumed value for 1000 MWe large nuclear power plant, the heat transfer to power ratio is 0.0028% and this is used as the reference value for normalization.
One can assume that 100% power ratio represents 1,000 MW large nuclear power plants, and the power level represents the nuclear reactor with different capacity. While the same power density is maintained and the power ratio decreases (i.e., the power capacity is reduced and becoming SMR), then the 300 MW SMR (i.e., 30% power ratio) will have 49% more available heat transfer to power due to the size effect. However, often many SMRs have lower power density than the large nuclear reactors since if the same power density is maintained then the inertia of the system can become too small where the control of the system will be jeopardized. Thus, for the case of reduced power density, the heat transfer to power ratio for 300 MW SMR will increase to 127% when the power density is a half, and 233% when the power density becomes 30% of 1000 MW class nuclear power reactor. Therefore, the increased surface area to volume ratio due to power capacity reduction and the reduction in power density in SMRs, the adoption of passive safety features becomes more favorable. The potential shortcoming of the approach for reducing the power density is that it reduces the advantages of an SMR physically being small, which is essential to the reduction in capital cost.
In terms of evaluating the radiological consequence of a severe accident in nuclear reactor, one first need to estimate the source term. Most of the operating nuclear reactors typically contain a fixed amount of fuel (i.e., radioactive material) in the reactor core to generate heat. The amount of radioactive material present in the reactor will be dependent on many factors. For example, if the power cycle efficiency is different between the nuclear power plants with the same electricity production capacity, then the reactor thermal power will be different, and therefore the amount of radioactive material stored in the reactor will be also different. Furthermore, if the reactor type is different and the enrichment of the fuel is different, then not only the quantity of the stored radioactive material in the reactor will be different but also the composition of the stored radioactive material will be different. To make the problem more complex, the same amount of radioactive material does not always have the same radiological consequence to the surrounding environment if the radioactive material composition is different, since the type and energy level of radiation from the material also greatly influences the radiological consequence. Therefore, it is a complicated process to precisely estimate how much radioactive material is stored in a nuclear reactor and understanding the potential radiological consequence that can be caused from a severe accident where the radioactive material can be released in an uncontrolled manner.
However, it is imperative for SMRs to demonstrate that the radiological consequence is small even for a severe accident that has very small frequency to occur. This is because the surrounding area which requires evacuation planning and population control (i.e., emergency planning zone (EPZ)) in case of radioactive material release is very much dependent on the maximum possible radiological consequence that can occur. In other words, for the SMR to be sited near the densely populated area, it is very important to show that the radiological consequence can be very small even for a very unlikely case, a severe accident.
The radiological consequence of the surrounding area is first dependent on how the radioactive material disperses from the source, which will be the severely damaged nuclear power plant. Thus, by observing how the dispersion model predicts the radioactive material transportation within the environment actually enlightens how small the reactor should be to minimize the area in control by the plant operator. If the area to be controlled is equivalent to the boundary of SMR building or exclusion area, then the economic burden will be minimized for the SMR owner and operator.
The dispersion of radioactive material in the environment depends on many factors and a complex model can be utilized to estimate the dispersion of radioactive material. However, in this study, instead of relying on a complex model, a simple Gaussian plume model is utilized to observe how much radiological consequence can be reduced by simply reducing the power of single unit. Figure 20 is a calculated result of dispersion factor obtained from the Gaussian plume model for continuous release shown in Eq. (5) with the following assumptions [16]:
\(\Gamma \) is the integrated source term (Bq), \(\chi \left(x,y,z,\right)\) is the integrated radioactivity concentration at the point in space (x,y,z) (Bq∙s/m3), u is the wind speed in the x direction and assumed as 2 m/s for conservative estimation, dispersion parameters \({\sigma }_{y},{\sigma }_{z}\) are determined from Pasquill stability class ‘F’ the Briggs urban model [16], \({\sigma }_{y}=0.11x{\left(1+0.0004x\right)}^{-0.5}\), \({\sigma }_{z}=0.08x{\left(1+0.0015x\right)}^{-0.5}\), h is the height of release and it is assumed to be zero in this study to estimate the plume on the ground.
If the reactors with similar technology are being compared, for example pressurized water reactor type, power level 100% in Fig. 20 represents 1000 MW class large nuclear reactors. Since 30 km in radius is the current emergency planning zone set by the Korean law, one can observe how the distance from the source to have the equivalent radiological consequence at 30 km for 1000 MW reactor changes with respect to the power capacity of nuclear reactor. This is shown in Fig. 20 for 50 MW, 100 MW, 200 MW, and 300 MW cases, shown as 5%, 10%, 20%, and 30% power level, respectively. Assuming that the thermal power is directly proportional to power capacity and radioactive material is linearly proportional to thermal power while having the same composition, the equivalent distance reduces with the power level. Figure 21 is plotted to show how the power level affects the equivalent distance. One can interpret that how EPZ can be reduced when power capacity decreases in a single unit of nuclear reactor if the EPZ is set to have the same radiological consequence at 30 km for 1000 MW reactor. It is shown in Fig. 21 that as the power of SMR reduces the equivalent distance reduction rate departs further from a linear reduction model which is unfavorable to SMRs.
Figures 20 and 21 show that if SMR owner or operator is aiming to reduce the EPZ, the size of EPZ is reduced quite linearly because of the reduction in power capacity. However, if EPZ is to be in the size of exclusion area (i.e., site boundary) which is in the order of a few hundred meters, the reduction in radiological material is not sufficient for SMRs to reduce EPZ. Safety features for reducing the source term further are necessary to reduce the total amount of radioactive material.
Why Is “Modular” Important?
Modular construction is equally an important concept for SMRs. The concept of modular reactor first implies that an SMR unit is manufactured mostly in the factory and can be constructed in modules so that the construction work can be minimized at the installation site. This approach is particularly important to make an SMR economical. If an SMR developer wants to depart from the economy of scale issue, then the manufacturing and construction methods have to become different from the conventional construction methods for large nuclear power plants [17]. In other words, since most of the large nuclear power plant project is similar to any chemical or energy plant construction projects, and if the SMR project is similar to the large nuclear power plant construction project, it will be very difficult to not follow the economy of scale, which means that the cost of electricity of SMR will be high and it will be difficult to be competitive with other energy systems. Therefore, in order to make an SMR more economical, the SMR project should be less similar to a plant construction project, but it should be closer to other type of project such as car or ship manufacturing industry where the final assembled product is transported to the user.
So how severe is the economy of scale issue? If one applies the widely used six-tenth rule for estimating the cost of nuclear power plants with similar design and technology but different capacity [17], the following figures demonstrate how the construction cost of SMR will behave. Equation (6) shows the simple relationship for approximately predicting the capital cost of plant with different power capacity [19, 20]:
C0 and C1 are the total capital cost of power plants having power P0 and P1, respectively. n is the cost factor and it will be 0.6 when six-tenth rule is applied and it will be unity when the cost has linear dependency to the scale.
Figure 22 shows the total construction cost reduction from 1000 MW nuclear power plant to 10 MW SMR. It is scaled relative to the 1000 MW nuclear power plant. The total construction cost will be reduced but not as fast as the construction cost estimated with linear assumption when six-tenth rule is applied. The linear assumption is assuming that the construction cost per MW is constant and the total construction cost is linearly dependent on the capacity. Figure 23 shows how the construction cost per MW changes with respect to the capacity of a single unit. Observing both figures, the construction cost of SMR per MW capacity will surely increase compared to 1000 MW large nuclear power plant if the SMR is manufactured and constructed with the similar technology.
For example, SMR with 300 MW capacity will be 52% cheaper in total construction cost but 62% expensive per MW basis. Therefore, using different nuclear reactor design from conventional large nuclear power plants, transforming manufacturing process, minimizing construction at the site, reducing the total construction period, and other innovative approaches will be all very important to avoid the economy of scale issue in SMRs.
Thus, modular construction represents the change in business approach of nuclear energy. It is the direction to transform nuclear energy project from plant construction project to manufacturing industry where mass production is the key to lower the cost. However, the concept of modular construction is clear but it is seldom realized in SMR development precisely and applied as a criterion for successful SMRs, since there will be always a construction work on the site even though the major components can be manufactured and assembled in a factory. The degree of construction work on site cannot be easily measured quantitatively as well. Furthermore, even the existing large nuclear reactors claim that it is being constructed with modular construction techniques. This means that a large section of a nuclear power plant is manufactured in the factory, transported to the site, and assembled at the site [21].
In summary, making an SMR small has advantages in terms of reducing the total construction cost to make nuclear energy much more affordable, improving safety due to the physical characteristics of SMR, simplifying the system to reduce the complexity of the system, and matching better with renewable energy due to the enhanced load following capability. However, the main challenge is to overcome the economy of scale which escalates the cost and thus the cost of final product can become high, and modularity is the key to success to move away from the economy of scale issue.
Necessary Technologies to Overcome Challenges
Reducing Source Term
As shown in the previous section, even though the total radioactive material in SMRs will be reduced and that can reduce the radiological consequence, but it is not enough to reduce the EPZ to the power plant site. Thus, additional technical measures are necessary to further reduce the source term so that in case of severe accident where radioactivity disperses to the surrounding environment in an uncontrolled manner [21].
First, passive safety systems are adopted to reduce the frequency of severe accident to occur. For LWR SMRs, some designs adopt steel vessel containment that can endure much higher pressure than the containment building in large nuclear power plants. This provides larger margin when the accident occurs, and passive cooling to the containment vessel is provided to reduce the probability of vessel failure. For non-LWR SMRs, reactor types utilizing coolants having high boiling temperature under atmospheric pressure, such as liquid metal-cooled reactors and molten salt reactors, high-pressure failure of containment structure is almost impossible to occur, and therefore, the source term leak rate will be very low. For gas-cooled reactors, nuclear fuel with multiple coatings such as TRISO is often used, and the coatings provide the containment function so that the release of radioactive material stored in the fuel can be minimized.
Reducing the source term via engineered safety features and successfully demonstrating the reduction in source term will be very important for the success of SMRs, since it will be one of the major factors determining the site for SMRs. If SMRs were to operate near the densely populated region to provide power or heat without long transmission line, the reduction in EPZ due to the reduced source term will be essential.
Reducing Manufacturing and Construction Cost
In order to avoid the economy of scale issue, the manufacturing process of SMRs have to be different from that of large nuclear power plants. The number of heavy equipment manufactured should be minimized, and the construction and manufacturing work at the site should be reduced as much as possible. The heavy equipment typically has a long lead time and also narrows the option for supply chain, which all translates to the increased capital cost. Furthermore, if SMRs also require significant amount of construction work at the site, the construction process will become very similar to a large nuclear power plant, and therefore, the economy of scale issue will be always there [10, 23].
In order to massively manufacture SMR in large amount, the site-dependent features have to be greatly reduced. Thus, SMRs should be designed to be immune to all possible external hazards those can be specific to the site. At the same time, the recent advancement in manufacturing technology should be aggressively adopted to reduce the manufacturing time as well as construction time at the site. Another way to reduce the construction cost of SMRs can be reusing the existing infrastructure of retiring coal or gas power plants, which can reduce the cost of SMR 15–35% [24]. Thus, designing an SMR to fully utilize the existing coal power plant infrastructure can be also another promising direction for designing successful SMRs.
Reducing the Number of Operation and Maintenance Personnel
SMRs cannot maintain the same number of operation and maintenance (O&M) personnel as large nuclear power plants do. If SMRs are designed to require similar O&M skills from the labor forces, then the number of people per module will be very unlikely to reduce since the number of people will be constant regardless of the power capacity. Therefore, SMRs should be designed to operate with reduced number of staffs for O&M, and the vendor has to successfully demonstrate to the licensing body that the safety of the SMRs will not be compromised due to the reduction in workforce.
In order to achieve the goal, autonomous control in conjunction with artificial intelligence (AI) and digital twin technologies will become imperative. These technologies are already being researched and developed in other engineering sectors [25, 26], and nuclear industries are also embracing these new developments. The autonomous control requires innovation from both hardware and software. In terms of hardware, SMRs should be designed to be simple as much as possible so that there are not many variables to control. For software, even though it will be too early to replace human from O&M completely, software assisting the O&M personnel should be developed to reduce the work load for individual. A digital twin of SMR can also provide the prediction of how system will degrade over time to optimize the maintenance and replacement of components, hence maximizing the capacity factor, which all translates to the economy of SMRs.
Reducing Uncertainties in Licensing
The uncertainties in licensing should be greatly reduced for the success of SMRs. SMRs should not be regulated as the same as large nuclear power plants, since many conservative assumptions were needed for the large nuclear power plants due to large consequence for very unlikely event. SMRs will undoubtedly have much smaller consequence than the large nuclear power plants and also the frequency of the unlikely event will become smaller as well. Combining these two effects the SMRs should not be regulated like large nuclear power plants, instead technically neutral and performance-based licensing approach should be adopted. The reform of licensing structure cannot be done by the regulatory body alone, since the regulatory body also requires information and feedback from the vendors to set up a framework with reasonable rule. Thus, this item should be worked together between the regulatory body and the SMR vendors [27].
Since SMRs can become economical only when they are produced massively, SMRs are expected to be installed in various regions. If SMRs have to be reviewed in terms of safety every time the SMR is planned to be constructed in a new country, the economics of SMR will be greatly deteriorated. In order to utilize SMRs for achieving carbon neutrality soon, the review process of SMRs has to be modernized and streamlined while checking all the important safety features and functions of SMRs. Therefore, the development on global standardized licensing for SMRs is also needed to further bring down the cost of SMRs [28].
Diversifying the Application Areas of SMRs
Decarbonization is an important issue not only for electricity production but all sectors involving human activities. So far, the nuclear energy has been utilized only in electricity generation in civilian sector. The advancement of SMRs can enable nuclear energy to be utilized in different sectors such as industry requiring heat, and transportation sector such as maritime transport for decarbonization in these sectors [29]. Furthermore, a new type of next-generation nuclear reactor is emerging to meet the demand of future electricity and heat application [30, 31], which proves the future potential of expanding the nuclear energy application.
As it was shown in the previous section, already a few SMR developers are collaborating with chemical industry or ship-building industry to widen the nuclear energy application area. This trend is expected to become more popular, and will undoubtedly make SMRs more versatile and promising. Furthermore, by applying nuclear energy to other sectors, not only nuclear energy can be utilized for the decarbonization of various industries, but also nuclear industry can learn advanced technologies from other industries to make nuclear energy more competitive.
Summary and Conclusions
This review highlights the pivotal role of SMRs and how SMRs will play a pivotal role in the future of nuclear power generation. The analysis juxtaposes traditional large-scale nuclear reactors with the emerging SMR technology, shedding light on the inherent advantages of SMRs in terms of economic feasibility, safety, and adaptability to diverse energy grids and applications. The journey of nuclear power from its inception over 70 years ago to the present day underscores a gradual shift from the pursuit of large-capacity plants to the innovative exploration of SMRs. This transition is driven by the limitations associated with large reactors, including the high financial burden of construction, the stringent requirements for capacities, and the escalated risks in the event of severe accidents. The advent of SMRs emerges as a strategic response to these challenges, offering a pathway to harness nuclear energy more flexibly and safely.
The paper reviewed various SMRs under development worldwide which has substantial implication to South Korea, highlighting their unique designs, safety enhancements, and potential for diverse applications. These advancements are set against the backdrop of the evolving energy market, which increasingly favors decentralization and diversification, further bolstered by the integration of renewable energy sources. A significant emphasis is placed on the intrinsic benefits of SMRs, particularly their smaller size and modular nature. These characteristics not only facilitate a reduction in the potential radiological impact of severe accidents but also enhance the feasibility of construction and operation in a wider array of locations, including regions with limited grid capacity. Moreover, the modular construction approach promises economic advantages by enabling mass production and reducing on-site construction work, thus potentially circumventing the economy of scale dilemma faced by traditional nuclear power projects.
The paper concludes by identifying key technological and regulatory strategies essential for overcoming the remaining hurdles to SMR deployment. These include innovations to minimize the source term, reduce manufacturing and construction costs, streamline operations and maintenance through automation, address licensing uncertainties, and diversify the application area. Collectively, these efforts underscore a collaborative path forward, necessitating active engagement between regulators, developers, and the broader nuclear industry. In summary, the transition towards SMRs represents a paradigm shift in nuclear power generation, promising to deliver safer, more flexible, and economically viable nuclear energy solutions. This evolution not only aligns with the global imperative for carbon neutrality but also offers a promising avenue to revitalize the nuclear sector by adapting to the rapidly changing energy landscape.
